infobox.key: United States Department of the Interior
infobox.value: (**DOI**) is an [executive department](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") of the [U.S. federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") responsible for the management and conservation of most [federal lands](/wiki/Federal_lands "Federal lands") and [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resources "Natural resources"). It also administers programs relating to [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives"), [Native Hawaiians](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians"), and four of the five inhabited [insular areas](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States") of the United States,[[4]](#cite_note-4) as well as programs related to historic preservation. About 75% of federal public land is managed by the department, with most of the remainder managed by the [Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture")'s [Forest Service](/wiki/United_States_Forest_Service "United States Forest Service").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The department was created on March 3, 1849. It is headquartered at the [Main Interior Building](/wiki/Main_Interior_Building "Main Interior Building"), located at 1849 C Street [NW](/wiki/Northwest,_Washington,_D.C. "Northwest, Washington, D.C.") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

The **United States Department of the Interior** (**DOI**) is an [executive department](/wiki/United_States_federal_executive_departments "United States federal executive departments") of the [U.S. federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") responsible for the management and conservation of most [federal lands](/wiki/Federal_lands "Federal lands") and [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resources "Natural resources"). It also administers programs relating to [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), [Alaska Natives](/wiki/Alaska_Natives "Alaska Natives"), [Native Hawaiians](/wiki/Native_Hawaiians "Native Hawaiians"), and four of the five inhabited [insular areas](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States") of the United States,[[4]](#cite_note-4) as well as programs related to historic preservation. About 75% of federal public land is managed by the department, with most of the remainder managed by the [Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture")'s [Forest Service](/wiki/United_States_Forest_Service "United States Forest Service").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The department was created on March 3, 1849. It is headquartered at the [Main Interior Building](/wiki/Main_Interior_Building "Main Interior Building"), located at 1849 C Street [NW](/wiki/Northwest,_Washington,_D.C. "Northwest, Washington, D.C.") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

The department is headed by the [secretary of the interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior"), who reports directly to the [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and is a member of the president's [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States "Cabinet of the United States"). The current interior secretary is [Doug Burgum](/wiki/Doug_Burgum "Doug Burgum"), who was sworn in on February 1, 2025.

As of mid-2004, the department managed 507 million acres (2,050,000 km2) of surface land, or about one-fifth of the land in the United States. It manages 476 dams and 348 [reservoirs](/wiki/Reservoir_(water) "Reservoir (water)") through the [Bureau of Reclamation](/wiki/Bureau_of_Reclamation "Bureau of Reclamation"), 433 [national parks](/wiki/National_park "National park"), monuments, historical sites, etc. through the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), and 544 national [wildlife refuges](/wiki/Wildlife_refuge "Wildlife refuge") through the [Fish and Wildlife Service](/wiki/Fish_and_Wildlife_Service "Fish and Wildlife Service"). The largest land management agency is the [Bureau of Land Management](/wiki/Bureau_of_Land_Management "Bureau of Land Management"), managing about one-eighth of the land in the United States.

Despite its name, the Department of the Interior has a different role from that of the [interior ministries](/wiki/Interior_ministry "Interior ministry") of other nations, which are usually responsible for police matters and internal security. In the United States, national security and immigration functions are performed by the [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security") primarily and the [Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") secondarily. The Department of the Interior has often been humorously called "the Department of Everything Else" because of its broad range of responsibilities.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Interior&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Formation of the department

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Interior&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation of the department")]

A department for domestic concern was first considered by the [1st United States Congress](/wiki/1st_United_States_Congress "1st United States Congress") in 1789, but those duties were placed in the [Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State"). The idea of a separate domestic department continued to percolate for a half-century and was supported by presidents from [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") to [James Polk](/wiki/James_Polk "James Polk"). The 1846–48 [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War") gave the proposal new steam as the responsibilities of the federal government grew. Polk's [secretary of the treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury"), [Robert J. Walker](/wiki/Robert_J._Walker "Robert J. Walker"), became a vocal champion of creating the new department.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 1849, Walker stated in his annual report that several federal offices were placed in departments with which they had little to do. He noted that the [United States General Land Office](/wiki/United_States_General_Land_Office "United States General Land Office") had little to do with the Treasury and also highlighted the [Indian Affairs office](/wiki/Bureau_of_Indian_Affairs "Bureau of Indian Affairs"), part of the [Department of War](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_War "United States Department of War"), and the [Patent Office](/wiki/United_States_Patent_and_Trademark_Office "United States Patent and Trademark Office"), part of the [Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State"). Walker argued that these and other bureaus should be brought together in a new Department of the Interior.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] A bill authorizing its creation of the department passed the [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") on February 15, 1849, and spent just over two weeks in the [Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). The department was established on March 3, 1849 (9 [Stat.](/wiki/United_States_Statutes_at_Large "United States Statutes at Large") [395](https://legislink.org/us/stat-9-395)), the eve of President [Zachary Taylor](/wiki/Zachary_Taylor "Zachary Taylor")'s inauguration, when the Senate voted 31 to 25 to create the department. Its passage was delayed by [Democrats](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") in [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") who were reluctant to create more [patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage") posts for the incoming [Whig](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") administration to fill. The first [secretary of the interior](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Interior "United States Secretary of the Interior") was [Thomas Ewing](/wiki/Thomas_Ewing "Thomas Ewing").

Several of the domestic concerns the department originally dealt with were gradually transferred to other departments. For example, the Department of Interior was responsible for water pollution control prior to the creation of the [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Other agencies became separate departments, such as the [Bureau of Agriculture](/wiki/Bureau_of_Agriculture "Bureau of Agriculture"), which later became the [Department of Agriculture](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture"). However, land and natural resource management, American Indian affairs, wildlife conservation, and territorial affairs remain the responsibilities of the Department of the Interior.

### Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Interior&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Controversies")]

Secretary of the Interior [Albert B. Fall](/wiki/Albert_B._Fall "Albert B. Fall") was implicated in the [Teapot Dome](/wiki/Teapot_Dome "Teapot Dome") scandal of 1921. He was convicted of bribery in 1929, and served one year in prison, for his part in the controversy. A major factor in the scandal was a transfer of certain oil leases from the jurisdiction of the [Department of the Navy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Navy "United States Department of the Navy") to that of the Department of the Interior, at Fall's behest.

Secretary of the Interior [James G. Watt](/wiki/James_G._Watt "James G. Watt") faced criticism for his alleged hostility to environmentalism, for his support of the development and use of federal lands by foresting, ranching, and other commercial interests, and for banning [the Beach Boys](/wiki/The_Beach_Boys "The Beach Boys") from playing a 1983 Independence Day concert on the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall") out of concerns of attracting "an undesirable element". His 1983 resignation was prompted by a speech in which he said about his staff: "I have a black, a woman, two Jews and a cripple. And we have talent."[[11]](#cite_note-bart-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Under the Administration of President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"), the Interior Department's maintenance backlog climbed from $5 billion to $8.7 billion, despite Bush's campaign pledges to eliminate it completely. Of the agency under Bush's leadership, Interior Department inspector general [Earl Devaney](/wiki/Earl_Devaney "Earl Devaney") has cited a "[culture of fear](/wiki/Culture_of_fear "Culture of fear")" and of "ethical failure." Devaney has also said, "Simply stated, short of a crime, anything goes at the highest levels of the Department of Interior."[[13]](#cite_note-lat012409-13)

Launched in June 2021, the Federal Indian Boarding School Initiative intended to investigate federal Indian boarding school policies and multi-generational impacts of trauma on American Indian, Alaska Native, and Native Hawaiian children. Released in two volumes, the three year investigation produced the first report[[14]](#cite_note-14) in May 2022 and the second and final[[15]](#cite_note-15) volume in June 2024. The final report details the severe trauma and cultural disruption inflicted on Native American communities through these schools, which operated from the late 19th century to the mid-20th century. It highlights the systemic abuse and neglect endured by students, finding 973 children died at the schools and calls for accountability and measures to address the ongoing impact on Native American families and communities to include working closely with tribal nations on the identification and repatriation of the remains.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Federal Consulting Group

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Interior&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Federal Consulting Group")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **may need to be cleaned up.** It has been [merged](/wiki/H:M "H:M") from *[Federal Consulting Group](/wiki/Federal_Consulting_Group "Federal Consulting Group")*. |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/47/Federal_consulting_group.png)](/wiki/File:Federal_consulting_group.png)

Logo

The Federal Consulting Group was a part of the United States Department of the Interior until March 2025 when it was dissolved on the advice of [Department of Government Efficiency](/wiki/Department_of_Government_Efficiency "Department of Government Efficiency") (DOGE). The group was paid to broker contracts for other parts of the federal government[[17]](#cite_note-17) as well as offering consulting, executive coaching, and performance and customer satisfaction measuring services to federal agencies.[[18]](#cite_note-usagov-18)

In 2009 it described itself as part of the [National Business Center](/wiki/National_Business_Center "National Business Center") of the DoI:

The Federal Consulting Group provides consultation services aimed at overcoming the organizational challenges of client offices. This is accomplished through assessment and construction of organizational logic models, amongst other methods. The Federal Consulting group consists of three divisions: Consulting, Executive Coaching, and Performance Measurement & Customer Satisfaction. The Consulting and Coaching divisions provide the services indicated by their titles, while the Performance Measurement & Customer Satisfaction division provides clients with access to the [American Customer Satisfaction Index](/wiki/American_Customer_Satisfaction_Index "American Customer Satisfaction Index") (ACSI). The ACSI allows federal offices to demonstrate compliance with the President's Management Agenda and the Government Performance and Results Act to the Administration and Congress.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In April 2021, the National Science Foundation Office of Polar Programs entered into an agreement with Federal Consulting Group to oversee a needs assessment of the USAP as relates to allegations of sexual harassment and assault.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The group's last address was 1849 C St. NW Room 4342, Washington, DC 20240-0001.[[18]](#cite_note-usagov-18)

## American Indians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Department_of_the_Interior&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: American Indians")]

Within the Interior Department, the [Bureau of Indian Affairs](/wiki/Bureau_of_Indian_Affairs "Bureau of Indian Affairs") handles some federal relations with American Indians, while others are handled by the Office of Special Trustee. The current assistant secretary for Indian affairs is William Kirkland, an enrolled member of the Navajo Nation.

The department has been the subject of disputes over proper accounting for American Indian Trusts set up to track the income and distribution of monies that are generated by the trust and specific American Indian lands, which the government leases for fees to companies that extract oil, timber, minerals, and other resources. Several cases have sought an accounting of such funds from departments within the Interior and Treasury (such as the Minerals Management Service), in what has been a 15-year-old lawsuit. Some American Indian nations have also sued the government over water-rights issues and their treaties with the US. In 2010 Congress passed the Claims Settlement Act of 2010 (Public Law 111-291), which provided $3.4 billion for the settlement of the *[Cobell v. Salazar](/wiki/Cobell_v._Salazar "Cobell v. Salazar")* class-action trust case and four American Indian water rights cases.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-Warren-22)

On March 16, 2021, [Deb Haaland](/wiki/Deb_Haaland "Deb Haaland"), serving at that time as a member of Congress for New Mexico, took the oath of office as secretary, becoming the first American Indian to lead an executive department, and the third woman to lead the department.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
